summary: The paper studies equilibrium computation in imperfect-recall extensive-form games (IREFGs), a setting in which players may forget parts of their history or previously acquired information and where computing equilibria is provably hard. The central idea is to reformulate IREFGs over behavioral strategies as polynomial optimization problems over products of simplices and to apply the Moment-Sum-of-Squares (Moment-SOS) hierarchy. For single-player IREFGs, the authors show that the hierarchy converges asymptotically to the ex-ante optimal value, converges at a finite level under a genericity assumption, and, for non-absentminded (NAM) games, converges exactly at a level bounded by the number of information sets by exploiting the multi-affine structure of NAM utilities. For the multi-player case, they adapt the Select-Verify-Cut (SVC) framework to either return a behavioral Nash equilibrium or certify its non-existence. Finally, they introduce the new classes of SOS-concave and SOS-monotone IREFGs, for which the hierarchy converges at the first level, so that equilibria can be computed with a single semidefinite program. Small-scale experiments illustrate that the SOS approach reliably attains global optima where a projected-gradient baseline does not.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
This is a solid and carefully executed contribution that establishes a rare positive algorithmic result for a setting -- equilibrium computation in imperfect-recall extensive-form games -- where even finding a local optimum can be NP-hard. The central idea, reformulating IREFGs over behavioral strategies as polynomial optimization over products of simplices and deploying the Moment-SOS hierarchy, is natural and elegantly bridges algorithmic game theory with polynomial optimization and real algebraic geometry. On soundness, the transformation is mathematically rigorous and the proofs I checked are clean and logically consistent (I did not verify the appendices in full detail). The observation that non-absentminded IREFGs induce multi-affine utility polynomials, which is then exploited to obtain exact finite convergence at a level bounded by the number of information sets, is a genuine and satisfying technical advance, and the SOS-concave and SOS-monotone subclasses that collapse to a single SDP at the first level are a clean and appealing characterization. The adaptation of the Select-Verify-Cut framework to the multi-player setting, yielding either a behavioral Nash equilibrium or a certificate of its non-existence, is also a nice contribution.

That said, several soundness-adjacent points deserve tightening. The finite-convergence results lean heavily on a genericity assumption; while it is true that degenerate instances form a measure-zero set, many games of interest have integer payoffs, pervasive ties, or symmetric structure that induce continua of equilibria (infinitely many KKT points) landing precisely on that measure-zero variety, so the assumption may be more restrictive in practice than the framing suggests. Moreover, the precise definition of genericity is somewhat buried and would benefit from an explicit statement in the main text. For the multi-player SVC procedure, the number of iterations can in principle grow with game size, and a worst-case analysis is absent.

Presentation is generally strong -- the paper is well structured, the equations are clear, and worked examples such as Figure 1 aid understanding -- but a large fraction is spent on definitions and setup with comparatively little intuition for the SOS machinery itself, and a reader new to moment-SOS methods would benefit from more guidance. There are also minor inconsistencies: the emphasis of definitions is not uniform, the symbol u in the definition of the ex-ante optimal behavioral strategy (around page 3, lines 147-148) appears to be used before it is properly introduced, and several author names in the references have broken or missing accents.

On significance and practicality, the main concern is scalability. The size of the SDPs underlying the hierarchy grows very quickly, so the computational cost of SOS-certified global optimality is high relative to cheaper probabilistic or gradient-based baselines, and the general-purpose value of the method beyond the identified tractable subclasses is not fully established. An analysis of the approximation quality when the hierarchy is truncated at a low, computationally feasible level -- before exact convergence -- would considerably strengthen the practical case, and is currently missing.

The empirical evaluation is the weakest part. Experiments are run only on very small instances, with reported running times between roughly 0.01 and 9 seconds, so larger instances appear to have been well within reach and would have better demonstrated both the empirical limits and the practical relevance of the approach. The comparison is limited to a single projected-gradient baseline; contrasting against MILP-based or other game-solving methods would be more informative. The test problems are also manually constructed polynomial utilities rather than genuine game-theoretic instances (for example, the absentminded-driver game or an imperfect-recall poker abstraction), which makes it hard to connect the numbers to the games the method is meant to solve. Finally, there is no stress test that identifies the boundary of tractability where the method exhausts time or memory.

On originality, the framework is a well-motivated and non-trivial synthesis, but the core device of casting single-player IREFGs as polynomial optimization and applying existing SOS/moment hierarchies builds substantially on established machinery; the more original elements are the multi-player SVC extension and the new SOS-concave/SOS-monotone classes. Overall, despite the practical and empirical limitations, the theoretical contribution is meaningful and the paper is a worthwhile addition to a difficult and under-explored area.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. Could you state the genericity conditions precisely in the main text, and discuss how prevalent they are across natural classes of games (e.g., team games) given that integer payoffs, ties, and symmetric structure can induce continua of equilibria on the measure-zero variety? Are there heuristics for handling such degenerate cases?
2. How common are SOS-concave and SOS-monotone games in real-world or naturally occurring settings, and what structural conditions make a game fall into these tractable subclasses?
3. Are there theoretical guarantees, or at least empirical observations, on the approximation error of the strategies extracted when the hierarchy is truncated at a low, computationally tractable level before exact convergence is reached?
4. Why was projected gradient descent chosen as the sole baseline? Could you compare against MILP-based formulations or other game-solving methods?
5. Can you evaluate the method on at least one genuine game instance (e.g., the absentminded-driver game or an imperfect-recall poker abstraction) and characterize the scalability boundary at which the approach runs out of time or memory?
limitations: The core limitations -- the restrictiveness of the genericity assumption and the exponential growth of the SDP size -- should be discussed more explicitly and prominently rather than only briefly touched upon, and the practical boundary of tractability should be stated transparently.
